Name: LatencyAwarePACBayesInference
Title: Latency-Aware PAC-Bayes Inference with Jacobian-Based Layer Skipping
Short Hypothesis: By integrating PAC-Bayes frameworks with Jacobian-based layer skipping, we can develop a training-free, latency-aware inference method that maintains high accuracy while significantly reducing inference time and memory usage.
Related Work: Existing research in PAC-Bayes frameworks primarily focuses on theoretical guarantees for model generalization and robustness. However, there is limited work on applying these frameworks to latency-critical inference scenarios. Recent work on layer skipping and lightweight models for real-time applications (e.g., [2]) has shown promise but lacks a rigorous theoretical foundation and empirical validation in latency-constrained settings. Our proposal fills this gap by combining PAC-Bayes stability bounds with Jacobian-based layer skipping to ensure both theoretical guarantees and practical efficiency.
Abstract: We propose a novel approach to latency-aware machine learning inference using PAC-Bayes frameworks and Jacobian-based layer skipping. This method integrates theoretical guarantees from PAC-Bayes stability bounds with practical, compute-efficient layer skipping to maintain high accuracy while significantly reducing inference time and memory usage. By deriving enforceable, strictly bounded-overhead protocols, we ensure that the model remains robust and efficient, even under strict latency constraints. Our approach is validated through a series of experiments on latency-critical tasks, demonstrating its effectiveness in real-world applications.
Experiments: We will implement a Jacobian-based layer-skipping mechanism in a deep neural network. The Jacobian matrix will be used to dynamically skip layers during inference based on the sensitivity of the hidden states to input perturbations. We will measure the inference time and memory usage with and without layer skipping to quantify the efficiency gains.

We will derive PAC-Bayes bounds for the model's performance and stability under layer skipping. The bounds will be used to enforce strict latency constraints while maintaining a high level of accuracy. We will evaluate the bounds on a variety of datasets and tasks to ensure their generalizability.

We will deploy the model on latency-critical tasks such as real-time image classification, object detection, and natural language processing. We will compare the latency and accuracy of our method against baseline models and state-of-the-art lightweight models. The evaluation will include both synthetic and real-world datasets to ensure robustness and practical applicability.

We will evaluate the scalability of our method by deploying it on different hardware configurations, including single-GPU and multi-GPU setups. We will measure the wall-clock time and memory usage to ensure that the method remains efficient and scalable across different compute tiers.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The Jacobian-based layer-skipping mechanism may introduce additional computational overhead for calculating the Jacobian matrix, which could offset some of the efficiency gains. We will carefully optimize this process to minimize the overhead.
The PAC-Bayes bounds may be overly conservative in some cases, leading to suboptimal layer skipping. We will explore techniques to refine the bounds and improve their practical utility.

The method's performance may vary across different types of neural networks and datasets. We will conduct extensive ablation studies to identify the most effective configurations and ensure robustness.

Deploying the method on real-world latency-critical tasks may reveal unforeseen challenges, such as hardware-specific limitations and data distribution shifts. We will address these issues through detailed empirical validation and adaptive strategies.
